title: Paid by the Ship
passage: For three centuries the small Baltic port of Vellmar entrusted its night-time safety to a guild of lantern-keepers, who were paid not by the town council but directly by the ships that used the harbour. A vessel that reached the quay safely paid the keepers a fixed toll; a vessel that ran aground paid nothing at all. The council's records show that it never once had to discipline a keeper for a darkened lantern. When the neighbouring town of Hesse-Aldern switched to salaried keepers funded from general taxes, its wreck rate climbed within a single decade, and its own chroniclers noted enviously that Vellmar's lamps never seemed to sleep. The Vellmar guild was finally dissolved in 1809, not for any failure of its own, but because a new state lighthouse authority quietly absorbed all of its duties.

questions.prompt: What does the passage suggest about why Vellmar's lanterns were so reliably kept?

options.text: The town council never once had to discipline a keeper for letting a lantern go dark.
Baltic ports of that era generally maintained far higher safety standards than inland towns did.
State-funded lighthouses were always less effective than privately financed lantern guilds.
Keepers were paid only when ships arrived safely, so keeping the lamps lit served their own interest.

questions.rationale: TYPE-002 inference one inch beyond the text. The passage states the payment structure (toll only on safe arrival, nothing on a wreck) and the outcome (never a dark lantern; the salaried rival's wrecks rose) but never names the incentive; D supplies exactly that one-step link. A = too_literal (restates a stated fact, the evidence, not the 'why'). B = two-step leap / outside_knowledge (unsupported regional generalisation). C = quantifier_upgrade ('always' over-broadens a single town-to-town comparison into a universal law).

generator_meta.originality_note: Vellmar, Hesse-Aldern, the guild and the quote 'never seemed to sleep' are all invented; 1809 date and 'state lighthouse authority' are generic period colour, no real person quoted.
